topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of stale coffee and old paper suddenly soured into something acrid and metallic. Liam froze in the doorway of his room, his hand still hovering near the doorknob. His eyes locked onto the open journal lying on his desk—his journal. The handwriting, his own frantic, unguarded scribbles from weeks ago, was meticulously copied, line for line, in a neat, almost clinical script on the page. A hot flush crept up his neck, turning his skin tight. He took a sharp, ragged breath, the air catching painfully in his chest. "What in the actual hell?" he managed, the sound thin and brittle. He slammed the door shut, the sound echoing like a gunshot in the small apartment. Every muscle in his jaw clenched so hard he thought his teeth might crack. He started pacing the small space, his movements jerky and sharp, his gaze darting from the desk to the closed door, a furious, trapped energy vibrating just beneath his skin.

[story 2]

Sarah felt a cold, sickening dread coil in her stomach as she rounded the corner into the living room. There, on the coffee table, lay her private diary, its pages spread open to a particularly vulnerable entry. Her breath hitched, a sudden, shallow gasp escaping her lips. When she saw the familiar loop of her own thoughts documented so plainly, a white-hot surge shot through her. Her knuckles went white where she gripped the edge of the sofa. "You absolute monster," she hissed, the words emerging low and shaking, laced with a dangerous tremor. She didn't move to pick it up; instead, she spun around, her eyes blazing with a fierce, uncontained intensity. Her posture stiffened, every line of her body coiled tight, radiating a palpable, aggressive tension that made the air around her feel thick and charged.

[story 3]

Mark found the evidence tucked beneath his pillow. The familiar spiral binding of his journal was there, but the contents were a violation. A deep, visceral churning started in his gut, quickly escalating into a violent tremor that ran from his core to his fingertips. He didn't scream; the sound felt too large, too uncontrolled. He dropped the book onto the floor with a heavy thud that seemed disproportionate to the action. His breathing became shallow and rapid, each inhale a struggle against a rising tide of pure, furious indignation. He stood rigid, his fists balled so tightly his nails dug crescents into his palms, his face contorted into a mask of raw, seething disbelief. He stared at his roommate, his jaw working silently, the silence between them screaming louder than any shout could have.
